what_worked_well: --delay 5 landed on the second every time: 3 trials at 5.031s / 5.034s / 5.038s, a tight ~31-38ms spread that matches DEMO.md's claimed '~40ms wake-driven' latency. As an operator I'd call this 'on the second'.

Scheduler idle CPU is genuinely zero, confirmed two independent ways: /proc/<sched>/stat utime+stime stayed 0->0 ticks over a 6s window WITH a --delay 8 armed and pending, AND top showed the scheduler bash row at %CPU=0.0 with system-wide 100.0 id. No busy-spin, no timer wheel churn -- exactly what the README/DEMO headline promises.

Zero-config start matched the persona expectation: 'bash src/shellmux serve $D --unix $SOCK' then sub then pub. No config file, no port registry, no service unit. DEMO.md Beat 2 is copy-paste accurate.

The filesystem-as-state introspection is delightful and honest: right after publish, 'ls deferred/' showed the pending deadline as a single filename 1782178990615.2991023967 (the documented <run_at_ms>.<seq> form); after the fire, deferred/ was empty -- you can literally watch the single mv commit point with ls.

--delay 1 fired at 1.031s, confirming the sub-second wake floor holds on the container's bash 5.2 (not the ~1s whole-second floor that bash3 would impose). The docs are careful to platform-qualify this and the behavior matched.
--at absolute-epoch form works through the same scheduler and fires at the absolute target, not delay-from-publish -- correct semantics.
wished_for: A built-in one-shot way to time a delayed delivery without hand-rolling a /proc CPU sampler -- e.g. a documented 'shellmux' verb or a tiny snippet in README (not just DEMO.md) showing the operator how to confirm '0% during the wait' on their own box. DEMO.md tells you top reads 0% but doesn't hand you the /proc tick recipe; I had to build it.

DEMO.md Beat 2 backgrounds 'serve' and 'sub' with '&' but does NOT mention that 'pub --delay N' BLOCKS for the whole N seconds. A first-time operator pasting Beat 2 verbatim will see their terminal 'hang' for 5s and may think it's wedged. One sentence ('pub blocks for the linger/delay; background it') would prevent that.

An at-rest 'next deadline' human-readable view -- 'ls deferred/' shows epoch-millis filenames; a 'cat'-able fires-in or fires-at-HH:MM:SS rendering would be friendlier for an operator eyeballing what's queued.

friction_points.description: DEMO.md Beat 2 shows 'echo reboot-now | bash src/shellmux pub control --delay 5 ...' as a foreground command without noting that pub BLOCKS for the entire delay window. The persona/recipe notes mention it, but the user-facing DEMO.md does not. An operator following DEMO.md verbatim will experience a 5s 'hang' and may misread it as a stuck broker.
friction_points.evidence: DEMO.md:54 'echo reboot-now | bash src/shellmux pub "$D" control --delay 5 --unix "$SOCK"' (no '&', no blocking note). Observed: my pub backgrounded fine and exited code 0 after the delay; foreground pasters would just wait 5s.
friction_points.workaround: Background the pub with '&' (which I did); the message still fires correctly at the deadline regardless.

friction_points.description: The headline operator claim is '~0% CPU during the wait', but the user-facing docs only say 'top shows ~0% the whole 5s' without giving a reproducible measurement recipe. To get hard evidence I had to find the sched.sh pid via pgrep and diff /proc/<pid>/stat utime+stime myself. A homelab operator who wants to TRUST the claim has to invent the measurement.
friction_points.evidence: DEMO.md:55 comment '# top shows ~0% the whole 5s'. I had to add: SCHED=$(pgrep -f sched.sh|head -1); awk '{print $14+$15}' /proc/$SCHED/stat before/after a sleep. Result: 0->0 ticks, 0.0000% -- claim is TRUE, just not turnkey to verify.
friction_points.workaround: Hand-rolled a /proc/<sched>/stat tick-diff sampler plus a top -bn1 snapshot; both confirmed 0%.

friction_points.description: Self-inflicted but worth recording for the eval recipe: a generic 'wait' after backgrounding a pub will block forever because the long-lived broker and subscriber are also background jobs. My first multi-trial harness stalled after trial 1 for 3 minutes and I had to docker kill it. This is a harness footgun, not a shellmux bug, but the validated recipe in the task brief doesn't warn that 'wait' must target the specific pub PID.
friction_points.evidence: First run printed 'trial 1: latency=5.035s' then hung; ps showed the container alive but output mtime frozen at 18:43:49 while clock read 18:46:36. Fix: 'wait "$PUB"' targeting only the pub PID made v2 complete in ~30s.
friction_points.workaround: Rewrote to 'wait "$PUB"' (specific PID) and added a host-side 'timeout 120 docker run ...' guard.

evidence_log: ENV: GNU bash 5.2.15 aarch64, nproc=14, clk_tck=100 (container shellmux-dev, image 09f797e9e437).

RUN1 pub control --delay 5 'reboot': T0=1782178985.609; deferred staged immediately as 'ls deferred/' -> 1782178990615.2991023967; subscriber saw 'reboot'; observed publish->subscriber latency=5.093s (incl my 100ms poll); deferred/ EMPTY after fire (single mv commit).
RUN1 scheduler CPU: /proc/16/stat utime+stime 0->0 over the wait = 0.0000 CPU-s; top -bn1 showed only idle socat rows at 0.0% CPU.
RUN1 pub exit code: 0 (clean exit after delay).
RUN2 (v2, precise 5ms poll, wait targeted at PUB pid): --delay 5 trials latency=5.031s, 5.034s, 5.038s (3/3 on the second).
RUN2: --delay 1 latency=1.031s (sub-second wake floor confirmed on bash 5.2).
RUN2: --at (epoch now+4) fired at absolute target (latency-from-publish 1.641s because ~1s sub-connect + clock rounding elapsed before pub ran -- correct absolute semantics).

RUN3 CPU-under-load: with --delay 8 armed (deferred/1782179290543.94736449 present), /proc/16/stat 0->0 ticks over 6s = 0.0000% of one core; top mid-wait sched bash row %CPU=0.0; system-wide %Cpu 100.0 id.
PRE-IDLE sample (RUN-v1): 6s pure-idle, no traffic -> sched ticks 0->0 = 0.0000% of one core.

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md / README: '--delay 5 message lands on the exact second'
claim_audit.evidence: 3 trials at 5.031/5.034/5.038s, ~31-38ms over target, matching the documented ~40ms wake latency. Lands on the second.

claim_audit.claim: 'top reads 0% the whole five seconds' / '~0% idle CPU, no timer wheel'
claim_audit.evidence: /proc/<sched>/stat 0->0 ticks over 6s with a delay armed = 0.0000% of one core; top showed sched %CPU=0.0 and 100.0 id system-wide. Confirmed two independent ways.

claim_audit.claim: 'the single commit point per due record is the mv ... deferred/ ... <run_at_ms>.<seq>'
claim_audit.evidence: ls deferred/ showed exactly one file 1782178990615.2991023967 while pending, and an EMPTY deferred/ after the fire -- consistent with a single atomic mv. (I did not and could not prove fire-once from one trial; that is the chaos test's job, not this challenge's.)

claim_audit.claim: '--at / --delay deferred delivery works through the scheduler'
claim_audit.evidence: Both --delay (1s,5s,8s) and --at (absolute epoch) fired through the same scheduler at the right time.

proof_assessment.note: This challenge is the LIVE single-trial face of the deadline proof, not the chaos gate. I verified the two operator-facing properties directly: (1) on-time delivery within documented resolution -- 3/3 --delay 5 trials at 5.031-5.038s, plus --delay 1 at 1.031s and --at firing at its absolute target; (2) ~0% scheduler CPU during the armed wait -- 0 /proc ticks over 6s and top %CPU=0.0, corroborated twice. The deferred file appears immediately on publish (stage-then-poke visible) and deferred/ empties after the fire (single mv). I did NOT exercise missed=0/dup=0 over N>=5000 or the must-fail negative control -- that is the chaos_deadline.sh gate, out of scope for this product-phase challenge and a different evaluator's job. So this is a clean live PASS of the timing+CPU claims, NOT a substitute for the chaos proof.
proof_assessment.missed_dup_observed: n/a for single-trial challenge (deferred/ empties cleanly, no observed duplicate or miss across 5 manual fires)

free_text:
As a homelab broker operator, this is the experience I wanted. I followed DEMO.md Beat 2 almost verbatim: 'serve' with just a dir and a --unix socket (zero config), a 'sub control', then 'pub control --delay 5 reboot'. The message landed at 5.031-5.038s across three trials -- on the second by any operator's standard, and consistent with the docs' ~40ms wake-driven latency claim. Critically, the CPU claim is REAL, not hand-waved: with a delay armed and pending, the scheduler bash process burned 0 CPU ticks over 6 seconds (0.0000% of one core), confirmed both via /proc/<pid>/stat tick-diff and a top snapshot showing the row at %CPU=0.0 and the system at 100.0 idle. No timer-wheel spin. The filesystem introspection is a genuine pleasure -- 'ls deferred/' shows the pending deadline as one epoch-millis filename, and it vanishes the instant it fires, so you can literally watch the commit with ls and cat. --delay 1, --delay 8, and --at <epoch> all fired correctly through the same path.

Friction was minor and mostly on docs/observability, not behavior. (1) DEMO.md Beat 2 shows 'pub --delay 5' as a foreground command but never says pub BLOCKS for the whole delay -- a first-timer pasting it will think their terminal hung. (2) The '~0% CPU' claim is true but the docs give you no recipe to verify it yourself; I had to find sched.sh via pgrep and diff /proc ticks by hand -- a turnkey snippet or a 'next deadline' cat-able view would close the trust gap. (3) One stall was entirely my own harness bug (a bare 'wait' blocking on the long-lived broker/sub jobs); fixed by targeting wait at the pub PID -- noting it so the eval recipe can warn future runs. None of these are product defects.

Net: the headline operator promise -- delayed message on time, box not spinning -- is delivered and independently verifiable. Score 9; the only reason it's not a 10 is the missing turnkey 'prove it's 0% / what's queued' affordance and the undocumented pub-blocks behavior. Note: I did NOT test the chaos missed=0/dup=0 over 5000 or the must-fail negative control -- that's the chaos gate, a different challenge; this is the live single-trial timing+CPU proof and it passes cleanly. Removed my four scratch scripts after the run; the remaining .eval-scratch files belong to other concurrent personas. Scripts I ran lived at <repo>/.eval-scratch/broker-operator-*.sh (now cleaned up). Docs audited: <repo>/README.md and <repo>/DEMO.md (Beat 2 lines 48-61).
